# Finding the smallest natural number $n$ such that $n^2 - n + 11$ has exactly four prime factors (counting multiplicity)

## Setting up the problem

I need to find the smallest natural number $n$ such that $n^2 - n + 11$ has exactly four prime factors counting multiplicity (i.e., $\Omega(n^2 - n + 11) = 4$).

## Systematic search

Let me compute $n^2 - n + 11$ for successive values of $n$ and factor each result.

| $n$ | $n^2 - n + 11$ | Factorization | # prime factors |
|-----|----------------|---------------|-----------------|

| 3 | 17 | 17 | 1 |

| 29 | 823 | 823 | 1 |
| 30 | 881 | 881 | 1 |

Let me continue and look for 4 prime factors:

| 31 | 941 | 941 | 1 |

| 35 | 1201 | 1201 | 1 |
| 36 | 1271 | $31 \times 41$ | 2 |
| 37 | 1343 | $17 \times 79$ | 2 |
| 38 | 1417 | $13 \times 109$ | 2 |
| 39 | 1493 | 1493 | 1 |
| 40 | 1571 | 1571 | 1 |
| 41 | 1651 | $13 \times 127$ | 2 |
| 42 | 1733 | 1733 | 1 |
| 43 | 1817 | $23 \times 79$ | 2 |
| 44 | 1903 | $11 \times 173$ | 2 |
| 45 | 1991 | $11 \times 181$ | 2 |
| 46 | 2081 | 2081 | 1 |
| 47 | 2173 | $41 \times 53$ | 2 |
| 48 | 2267 | 2267 | 1 |
| 49 | 2363 | $17 \times 139$ | 2 |
| 50 | 2461 | $23 \times 107$ | 2 |
| 51 | 2561 | 2561 = $7? $ No. $2561 = 2561$. Check: $13 \times 197 = 2561$. | 2 |
| 52 | 2663 | prime | 1 |
| 53 | 2767 | 2767 | 1 |
| 54 | 2873 | $13 \times 221 = 13 \times 13 \times 17$ | 3 |
| 55 | 2981 | $11 \times 271$ | 2 |
| 56 | 3091 | $11 \times 281$ | 2 |
| 57 | 3203 | $3203 = 47 \times 68 +7?$ Actually $3203 = 59 \times?$ no. Checking: $3203/11=291.2$, $3203/13=246.4$, $3203/17=188.4$, $3203/23=139.3$, $3203/29=110.4$, $3203/31=103.3$, $3203/37=86.6$, $3203/41=78.1$, $3203/43=74.5$, $3203/47=68.1$, $3203/53=60.4$, $\sqrt{3203}\approx 56.6$. So prime. | 1 |
| 58 | 3317 | $31 \times 107$ | 2 |
| 59 | 3433 | $3433/11=312.1$, $3433/13=264.1$, $3433/17=201.9$, $3433/23=149.3$, $3433/29=118.4$, $3433/31=110.7$, $3433/37=92.8$, $3433/41=83.7$, $3433/43=79.8$, $3433/47=73.0$ → $47 \times 73 = 3431$ no. $3433/53=64.8$, $3433/59=58.2$, $\sqrt{3433}\approx58.6$. Prime. | 1 |
| 60 | 3551 | $11 \times 17 \times 19$ | 3 |

Wait, let me check $n=60$: $3600 - 60 + 11 = 3551$. $3551/11 = 322.8..$ no. $3551/17 = 208.9$. Let me recompute: $3551/11 = 322.8$. Not divisible. $3551/7 = 507.3$. $3551/13 = 273.2$. $3551/19 = 186.9$. $3551/23 = 154.4$. $3551/29 = 122.4$. $3551/31 = 114.5$. $3551/37 = 96.0 \to 37 \times 96 = 3552$. No. $3551/41 = 86.6$. $3551/43 = 82.6$. $3551/47 = 75.6$. $3551/53 = 67.0 \to 53 \times 67 = 3551$! Yes! So 2 prime factors.

Let me look more carefully for cases with 3 or 4 prime factors. Let me check some known values where $n^2-n+11$ is divisible by $11^2 = 121$. This happens when $n \equiv 0 \pmod{11}$ (since $n^2-n+11 = n(n-1)+11$, and if $n=11k$, then $n^2-n+11 = 11k(11k-1)+11 = 11(k(11k-1)+1) = 11(11k^2-k+1)$, divisible by $11$ once; divisible by $11^2$ when $11k^2-k+1 \equiv 0 \pmod{11}$, i.e., $-k+1 \equiv 0$, $k \equiv 1 \pmod{11}$, so $n = 11, 132, ...$).

At $n=11$: $121 = 11^2$ (2 factors).

Let me check $n = 132$: $132^2 - 132 + 11 = 17424 - 132 + 11 = 17303$. $17303/121 = 143.0$. $143 = 11 \times 13$. So $17303 = 11^3 \times 13$. That's 4 prime factors! But let me check if something smaller works first.

Let me be more systematic. I need to check all values more carefully for 4 prime factors.

Let me continue from where I had 3 prime factors:

$n=28$: $767 = 11 \times 17 \times 41$ — wait, $17 \times 41 = 697$, $11 \times 697 = 7667$. That's not right. $28^2 - 28 + 11 = 784 - 28 + 11 = 767$. $767/11 = 69.7$. Not divisible by 11. $767/13 = 59$. So $767 = 13 \times 59$. That's 2 prime factors. Let me redo this more carefully.

Let me recompute more carefully for key values:

$n=54$: $2916 - 54 + 11 = 2873$. $2873/13 = 221$. $221 = 13 \times 17$. So $2873 = 13^2 \times 17$. That's 3 prime factors.

$n=60$: $3600 - 60 + 11 = 3551$. $3551 = 53 \times 67$. 2 factors.

Let me try to look for values where the expression equals a product of 4 primes. The smallest such products would be things like $11^4 = 14641$, $11^3 \times 13 = 17303$, $11^2 \times 13^2 = 20449$, $11^2 \times 13 \times 17 = 26741$, etc.

Actually, let me try a different approach. Let me just systematically check more values:

For each $n$, I'll compute $f(n) = n^2 - n + 11$ and count prime factors with multiplicity.

I'll check more values starting from the 60s:

$n=61$: $3721-61+11=3671$. Is this prime? $3671/11=333.7$, $/13=282.4$, $/17=215.9$, $/19=193.2$, $/23=159.6$, $/29=126.6$, $/31=118.4$, $/37=99.2$, $/41=89.5$, $/43=85.4$, $/47=78.1$, $/53=69.3$, $/59=62.2$, $/61=60.2$. $\sqrt{3671}\approx60.6$. Prime.

$n=62$: $3844-62+11=3793$. $/11=344.8$, $/13=291.8$, $/17=223.1$, $/19=199.6$, $/23=164.9$, $/29=130.8$, $/31=122.4$, $/37=102.5$, $/41=92.5$, $/43=88.2$, $/47=80.7$, $/53=71.6$, $/59=64.3$, $/61=62.2$. $\sqrt{3793}\approx61.6$. Prime.

This is getting tedious. Let me focus on values of $n$ where $f(n)$ is divisible by small primes like 11, 13, 17, etc.

$f(n) \equiv 0 \pmod{11}$: $n^2 - n + 11 \equiv n^2 - n \equiv n(n-1) \equiv 0 \pmod{11}$, so $n \equiv 0$ or $1 \pmod{11}$.

$f(n) \equiv 0 \pmod{13}$: $n^2 - n + 11 \equiv 0 \pmod{13}$. $n^2 - n - 2 \equiv 0 \pmod{13}$. $(n-2)(n+1) \equiv 0 \pmod{13}$. So $n \equiv 2$ or $n \equiv 12 \pmod{13}$.

$f(n) \equiv 0 \pmod{17}$: $n^2 - n + 11 \equiv n^2 - n - 6 \equiv (n-3)(n+2) \equiv 0 \pmod{17}$. So $n \equiv 3$ or $n \equiv 15 \pmod{17}$.

$f(n) \equiv 0 \pmod{23}$: $n^2 - n + 11 \equiv n^2 - n - 12 \equiv (n-4)(n+3) \equiv 0 \pmod{23}$. So $n \equiv 4$ or $n \equiv 20 \pmod{23}$.

$f(n) \equiv 0 \pmod{29}$: $n^2 - n + 11 \equiv n^2 - n - 18 \equiv 0 \pmod{29}$. Discriminant: $1 + 72 = 73 \equiv 73 - 58 = 15 \pmod{29}$. Is 15 a QR mod 29? $15^{14} \pmod{29}$. $15^2=225=225-217=8$. $8^2=64=64-58=6$. $6^2=36=7$. $7^2=49=49-29=20$. $20^2=400=400-13\times29=400-377=23$. $23^2=529=529-18\times29=529-522=7$. So $15^{14} = 15^2 \cdot (15^2)^6 = $ hmm let me just compute: $15^{14} = (15^2)^7 = 8^7$. $8^2=6$, $8^4=36=7$, $8^7=8^4\cdot8^2\cdot8=7\cdot6\cdot8=336=336-11\times29=336-319=17$. Not 1, so 15 is not a QR mod 29. So $f(n)$ is never divisible by 29.

$f(n) \equiv 0 \pmod{31}$: $n^2 - n + 11 \equiv n^2-n-20 \equiv (n-5)(n+4) \equiv 0 \pmod{31}$. So $n \equiv 5$ or $n \equiv 27 \pmod{31}$.

$f(n) \equiv 0 \pmod{37}$: $n^2-n+11 \equiv n^2-n-26 \equiv 0 \pmod{37}$. Disc: $1+104=105 \equiv 105-74=31 \pmod{37}$. $31^{18} \pmod{37}$: $31^2=961=961-25\times37=961-925=36\equiv -1$. So $31^{18}=(-1)^9=-1\equiv 36$. Not 1. So no solutions mod 37.

$f(n) \equiv 0 \pmod{41}$: $n^2-n+11 \equiv n^2-n-30 \equiv (n-6)(n+5) \equiv 0 \pmod{41}$. So $n \equiv 6$ or $n \equiv 36 \pmod{41}$.

$f(n) \equiv 0 \pmod{43}$: $n^2-n+11 \equiv n^2-n-32 \equiv 0 \pmod{43}$. Disc: $1+128=129=129-86=43\equiv 0$. So discriminant is 0! $n = (1\pm 0)/2$. Need $2^{-1} \pmod{43} = 22$. So $n \equiv 22 \pmod{43}$.

$f(n) \equiv 0 \pmod{47}$: $n^2-n-36 \equiv (n-7)(n+...$ wait. $n^2-n+11-47=n^2-n-36$. Hmm, does this factor? Disc: $1+144=145 \equiv 145-3\times47=145-141=4 \pmod{47}$. $\sqrt{4}=2$. So $n=(1\pm 2)/2$. $n=3/2$ or $n=-1/2$. $2^{-1}=24 \pmod{47}$. $n=3\times24=72\equiv 72-47=25$ or $n=-24\equiv 23 \pmod{47}$.

$f(n) \equiv 0 \pmod{53}$: $n^2-n+11-53=n^2-n-42$. Disc: $1+168=169=13^2$. $n=(1\pm13)/2$. $n=7$ or $n=-6$. So $n\equiv 7$ or $n\equiv 47 \pmod{53}$.

OK so now I know which $n$ give $f(n)$ divisible by various primes. For 4 prime factors, I need $f(n)$ to be divisible by several primes or by high powers.

Let me look for $n$ where $f(n)$ is divisible by both 11 and 13:
$n \equiv 0$ or $1 \pmod{11}$ AND $n \equiv 2$ or $12 \pmod{13}$.

By CRT:
- $n \equiv 0 \pmod{11}, n \equiv 2 \pmod{13}$: $n \equiv 54 \pmod{143}$ (since $11\times 2 \times 6 = 132$... let me solve properly. $n=11k$, $11k\equiv 2\pmod{13}$, $k\equiv 2\times 6 = 12\pmod{13}$, $n=11(13m+12)=143m+132$. Hmm, $n=132$? But also $n \equiv 132 \pmod{143}$. And we computed $f(132) = 11^3\times 13$. 

Wait but we need *smallest* $n$. Let me check smaller solutions.

- $n \equiv 0 \pmod{11}, n \equiv 12 \pmod{13}$: $11k \equiv 12 \pmod{13}$, $k \equiv 12 \times 6 = 72 \equiv 7 \pmod{13}$. $n = 11(13m+7) = 143m+77$. So $n = 77$: $f(77) = 5929 - 77 + 11 = 5863$. $5863/11 = 533$. $533/13 = 41$. So $f(77) = 11 \times 13 \times 41$. That's 3 prime factors.

- $n \equiv 1 \pmod{11}, n \equiv 2 \pmod{13}$: $11k+1\equiv 2\pmod{13}$, $11k\equiv 1\pmod{13}$, $k\equiv 6\pmod{13}$. $n=11\times 6+1 = 67$? Wait, $n = 11(13m+6)+1 = 143m+67$. So $n=67$: $f(67) = 4489-67+11 = 4433$. $4433/11 = 403 = 13 \times 31$. So $f(67) = 11 \times 13 \times 31 = 4433$. That's 3 prime factors.

- $n \equiv 1 \pmod{11}, n \equiv 12 \pmod{13}$: $11k+1\equiv 12\pmod{13}$, $11k\equiv 11\pmod{13}$, $k\equiv 1\pmod{13}$. $n = 11(13m+1)+1 = 143m+12$. So $n=12$: $f(12) = 143 = 11\times 13$. 2 factors.

Now let me look for $n$ where $f(n)$ is divisible by 11 and 17:
- $n \equiv 0$ or $1 \pmod{11}$ AND $n \equiv 3$ or $15 \pmod{17}$.

$n \equiv 0 \pmod{11}, n \equiv 3 \pmod{17}$: $11k \equiv 3 \pmod{17}$, $k \equiv 3 \times 14 = 42 \equiv 8 \pmod{17}$. (Since $11^{-1} \equiv 14 \pmod{17}$: $11\times 14 = 154 = 9\times17+1$. Yes.) $n = 11(17m+8) = 187m + 88$. So $n=88$: $f(88) = 7744 - 88 + 11 = 7667 = 11 \times 697 = 11 \times 17 \times 41$. 3 prime factors.

$n \equiv 0 \pmod{11}, n \equiv 15 \pmod{17}$: $11k\equiv 15\pmod{17}$, $k\equiv 15\times 14 = 210\equiv 210-12\times17=210-204=6\pmod{17}$. $n=11(17m+6)=187m+66$. $n=66$: $f(66) = 4356-66+11 = 4301$. $4301/11 = 391 = 17\times 23$. So $f(66) = 11\times 17\times 23$. 3 prime factors!

$n \equiv 1 \pmod{11}, n \equiv 3 \pmod{17}$: $11k+1\equiv 3\pmod{17}$, $11k\equiv 2\pmod{17}$, $k\equiv 2\times14=28\equiv 11\pmod{17}$. $n=11(17m+11)+1=187m+122$. $n=122$: $f(122)=14884-122+11=14773$. $14773/11=1343=17\times79$. So $f(122)=11\times17\times79$. 3 factors.

$n \equiv 1 \pmod{11}, n \equiv 15 \pmod{17}$: $11k+1\equiv 15\pmod{17}$, $11k\equiv14\pmod{17}$, $k\equiv14\times14=196\equiv 9\pmod{17}$ ($196=11\times17+9$). $n=11(17m+9)+1=187m+100$. $n=100$: $f(100)=10000-100+11=9911$. $9911/11=901=17\times53$. So $f(100)=11\times17\times53$. 3 factors.

OK so cases divisible by two distinct primes among 11, 13, 17 are giving 3 factors typically (unless the quotient is also composite).

For 4 prime factors, I need:
1. Divisible by three distinct primes, with quotient also having a prime factor, OR
2. Divisible by a prime squared, plus two more primes, OR
3. Divisible by a prime cubed plus one more prime (like $n=132$: $11^3 \times 13$).

Let me look for $n$ where $f(n)$ is divisible by 11, 13, and 17 simultaneously:
$n \equiv 0$ or $1 \pmod{11}$, $n \equiv 2$ or $12 \pmod{13}$, $n \equiv 3$ or $15 \pmod{17}$.

That's $2^3 = 8$ combinations. The modulus is $\text{lcm}(11,13,17) = 2431$.

For $n$ small enough, let me check: we need $f(n) \geq 11 \times 13 \times 17 = 2431$. $f(n) = n^2 - n + 11 \geq 2431 \Rightarrow n^2 - n - 2420 \geq 0 \Rightarrow n \geq 50$ approximately.

Let me solve the CRT for smaller values. Actually, we had $f(66) = 11 \times 17 \times 23$ (3 factors) and $f(77) = 11 \times 13 \times 41$ (3 factors). We need something with 4 factors.

Let me look for $n$ where $f(n)$ is divisible by $11^2 = 121$:
$f(n) \equiv 0 \pmod{121}$. $n(n-1) + 11 \equiv 0 \pmod{121}$. $n(n-1) \equiv -11 \pmod{121}$. 

When $n \equiv 0 \pmod{11}$, say $n = 11a$: $11a(11a-1) + 11 \equiv 0 \pmod{121}$. $11a(11a-1) = 121a^2 - 11a$. So $121a^2 - 11a + 11 \equiv 0 \pmod{121}$. This gives $-11a + 11 \equiv 0 \pmod{121}$, i.e., $11(1-a) \equiv 0 \pmod{121}$, so $1-a \equiv 0 \pmod{11}$, $a \equiv 1 \pmod{11}$. So $n = 11(11m+1) = 121m + 11$. Values: $n = 11, 132, 253, ...$

When $n \equiv 1 \pmod{11}$, say $n = 11a+1$: $(11a+1)(11a) + 11 = 121a^2 + 11a + 11 \equiv 0 \pmod{121}$. So $11a + 11 \equiv 0 \pmod{121}$, $11(a+1) \equiv 0 \pmod{121}$, $a \equiv -1 \equiv 10 \pmod{11}$. $n = 11(11m+10)+1 = 121m + 111$. Values: $n = 111, 232, ...$

So for $11^2 | f(n)$: $n \equiv 11 \pmod{121}$ or $n \equiv 111 \pmod{121}$.

$n=11$: $f(11) = 121 = 11^2$. 2 factors.
$n=111$: $f(111) = 12321 - 111 + 11 = 12221$. $12221/121 = 101.0$. Yes, $12221 = 121 \times 101 = 11^2 \times 101$. 3 prime factors.
$n=132$: $f(132) = 17424 - 132 + 11 = 17303$. $17303/121 = 143 = 11\times 13$. $f(132) = 11^3 \times 13$. 4 prime factors!

But $n=132$ might not be the smallest. Let me look at other avenues.

Let me look for $n$ where $f(n)$ is divisible by $13^2 = 169$:
$n \equiv 2$ or $12 \pmod{13}$. Let $n = 13a + 2$: $f(n) = (13a+2)(13a+1) + 11 = 169a^2 + 39a + 2 + 11 = 169a^2 + 39a + 13$. For divisibility by 169: $39a + 13 \equiv 0 \pmod{169}$, $13(3a+1) \equiv 0 \pmod{169}$, $3a+1\equiv 0 \pmod{13}$, $a \equiv -1/3 \equiv (-1)(9) = -9 \equiv 4 \pmod{13}$. So $n = 13(13m+4)+2 = 169m + 54$. Values: $n = 54, 223, ...$

$n=54$: $f(54) = 2873 = 13^2 \times 17$. 3 factors.
$n=223$: $f(223) = 49729 - 223 + 11 = 49517$. $49517/169 = 293.0$. Is 293 prime? Yes. So $13^2 \times 293$. 3 factors.

Let $n = 13a + 12$: $f(n) = (13a+12)(13a+11) + 11 = 169a^2 + 299a + 132 + 11 = 169a^2 + 299a + 143$. $143 = 11\times13$. For div by 169: $299a + 143 \equiv 0 \pmod{169}$. $299 = 169 + 130$. So $130a + 143 \equiv 0 \pmod{169}$. $130a \equiv -143 \equiv 26 \pmod{169}$. $130a \equiv 26 \pmod{169}$. Divide by $\gcd(130, 169) = 13$: $10a \equiv 2 \pmod{13}$, $a \equiv 2/10 = 2 \times 4 = 8 \pmod{13}$ (since $10^{-1} \equiv 4 \pmod{13}$). $n = 13(13m+8)+12 = 169m + 116$. Values: $n = 116, 285, ...$

$n=116$: $f(116) = 13456-116+11 = 13351$. $13351/13 = 1027.0$. $1027/13 = 79$. So $f(116) = 13^2 \times 79$. 3
